_purpose: PRE-REGISTERED predictions for the AUTHORITY gate (egress + destructive-mutation), frozen BEFORE the authority A/B numbers exist. Builds on injection-taxonomy-prereg.json (same frozen class labels).

gate.egress: block send/share/invite whose destination is not grounded in the user's trusted task (unchanged from toolgate)
gate.mutation: block destructive irreversible actions (delete_file, delete_email, cancel_calendar_event) UNLESS the user's trusted task expresses destructive intent (delete/remove/cancel/clear/erase). The agent has no human-confirm channel, so an injected delete is blocked.

predictions_vs_toolgate.delete_only: NOW EXPECTED contained (this is the gap toolgate could not cover; the mutation gate is its jurisdiction)
predictions_vs_toolgate.egress_plus_delete: unchanged or stronger: contained (egress blocked; and the delete step now also blocked)
predictions_vs_toolgate.egress_mass_recipient: unchanged: contained, still flags benign over-block risk

utility_risk_to_measure: The mutation gate may block BENIGN destructive tasks whose user prompt lacks an explicit deletion keyword. Benign-utility regressions vs baseline are the honest cost and MUST be reported.
scoped_claim: Within a declared action taxonomy (egress + destructive mutation) and a task-grounded authorisation policy, Simurgh blocks unauthorised egress AND unauthorised destructive side-effects even when the live model follows injected instructions.
non_claim: Not jailbreak immunity; not prevention of injection; not coverage of non-destructive mutation (create/modify/reschedule) or financial/code actions. Those remain future capability-kernel families. The model may still be fooled.
